new_findings.title: Anchor the PPV ladder at $15 entry point to minimize first-purchase friction

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

sources.quote: you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber
price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries

you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP — identified as #1 creator concern). This is the clearest external data point available for building Palm's first PPV pricing SOP. The doubling structure is a concrete starting model.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this ladder structure as the baseline for Palm's PPV pricing SOP: 6 tiers starting at $15, roughly doubling each step. Document it formally in §7 pricing docs. Test against the external chat team's current pricing to see if a ladder approach could increase per-fan revenue. This also gives Palm a concrete pricing framework to share with creators during onboarding (addressing the #1 creator concern).

new_findings.title: Run a structured onboarding call covering 6 key areas before going live

variants.claim: A thorough onboarding call is the single most important trust-building moment and should cover mindset, content expectations, timeline, invoicing, credentials, and Q&A.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a dedicated onboarding call and systematically cover: (1) model's mindset and expectations, (2) content volume needed upfront and why, (3) realistic 3-month results timeline, (4) invoicing schedule explanation, (5) credential handover for socials and OF, (6) open Q&A floor for the model. They treat the call as a formal first impression, not a casual chat.

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

sources.quote: schedule that initial onboarding call and run it with the model this is that first impression
you need to establish some sort of a timeline and expectations as to when they should be able to receive the results
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.
if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (name, email, commission %, contract, voice memo) per §6 — it is async and document-driven. There is no documented structured live onboarding call. The form captures admin data but does not walk the creator through expectations, timelines, or content strategy.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a mandatory video onboarding call as Step 2 after the contract wizard. Build a call checklist (Notion/Airtable template) covering the 6 areas above. Record it for reference. This directly addresses the creator-trust gap implied by §6's thin onboarding and supports retention (§9).

new_findings.title: Never send mass PPVs — individual messaging generates 3-4x more revenue

variants.claim: Mass PPVs lose far more money than they make because the non-buyers (majority) could have been converted through individual, personalized outreach.
variants.what_they_do: They avoid mass PPV blasts entirely. Instead, chatters message each subscriber individually, open with small talk to warm them up, then present content as if it was created specifically for that fan. The argument: of 1,000 subscribers who receive a $20 mass PPV, ~50 buy; but of the 950 who didn't, 100–200 more could be closed through 1-on-1 conversation — revenue that is permanently lost with mass sends.

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: if you send mass ppvs yes you will make some money... the problem is you lose way more money than you actually do
it is never and trust me on that never a good idea to send mass ppvs... just chat with them yourself you will make 10 times the money minimum
people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP on PPV delivery method. No documented position on mass vs. individual PPV exists. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, institute a hard policy against mass PPV blasts. Default all PPV delivery to individual chat sequences. Use this framing in chatter training: mass PPV = lazy; individual = 10x revenue floor.

new_findings.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.
We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Send 1–2 free teaser images before any PPV offer to prime the subscriber

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Live-call training with seconds-fast feedback loops and single-point focus

variants.claim: The fastest way to improve a new chatter is to sit on a live call with them while they chat, give feedback within seconds, and focus on fixing only one thing at a time.
variants.what_they_do: Manager joins a live call with the chatter while they work in real time. Feedback is immediate ('stop, do it again'). Manager identifies the single highest-leverage improvement point and works only on that until fixed, then moves to the next. Chatters are not 'saved' — manager tells them what to do but makes them execute it themselves so the learning sticks.

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: Fast feedback loops. If you want someone to learn something quickly, you need there to be a short gap between when they do something and when they get feedback on it. And I mean seconds
Don't step in and do it for them. You can tell them what to do. That's how they actually learn
We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter training exists (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this as the core training protocol for first 2–4 weeks of any new in-house chatter. Build a simple tracker: one 'current focus point' per chatter updated weekly so managers don't multi-task the coaching.

new_findings.title: Weekly async Loom chat reviews focused on new subscribers + high-ticket purchases

variants.claim: Regular async video reviews (via Loom) of specific conversation types — new subscribers, purchases, high-ticket sales — plus chatter-submitted chats drive measurable skill improvement.
variants.what_they_do: Manager opens the 'new subscribers' tab and reviews those conversations each week. Also reviews purchase logs and high-ticket sales. Asks each chatter to submit specific chats for review. Records Loom feedback videos. Per-review focus stays on one improvement point. Maintains 'scientist mode' (critical, constructive) not 'dictator mode' (anger-driven). Each chatter gets one weekly improvement point plus actionable steps.

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.

sources.quote: We open up the new subscribers tab and read those conversations. We look at purchases. We look for high ticket sales. And we ask chatters every week to give us specific chats to review
Every week we analyze the one point of improvement for each chatter
For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal review cadence at Palm (§8, outsourced). Partially relevant to the AI chatting project as well. Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a weekly review ritual into the in-house chat SOP from day one: manager records a ≤5-min Loom per chatter, logs the single focus point and action steps in Airtable (add a 'Chatter Reviews' table under the chat team's workspace).

new_findings.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

sources.quote: take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words

some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) and there is no documented persona-briefing process internally. When Palm builds in-house or AI chatting, voice consistency will be critical — especially for AI personas (§3) where breaking character destroys the illusion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'chatter voice card' field to the Creators table in Airtable (and AI Account Profile for AI personas). Populate: age, typing quirks, signature emojis, pet names, niche. Feed this card to any chatter (human or AI) at session start. Especially high priority for AI persona chatting.

new_findings.title: Hold weekly video calls with each creator to give account updates and collect feedback

variants.claim: A recurring weekly Zoom/video call with the model — showing results, flagging problems honestly, and requesting content adjustments — is the primary relationship-maintenance mechanism.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a standing weekly call per model on a video platform (Zoom or equivalent). The call covers: current account performance update (good and bad news), requested content changes for the coming week, and any operational issues. The visual face-to-face element is cited as relationship-strengthening beyond what async messaging achieves.

variants.claim: Agencies that hide negative account developments lose model trust catastrophically when the model discovers the issue independently; proactive disclosure, even of bad news, strengthens the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Their agency policy is to give updates on account performance whether good or bad. If something goes wrong (traffic drop, platform issue, content underperformance), they inform the model directly rather than waiting or papering over it. This is presented as a cultural value established from onboarding onwards.

sources.quote: set up a Cadence of calls like on a weekly basis where you get on a zoom or get on some sort of call with the model you see them face to face and you explain what's going on
you don't want to just make it seem like everything's going perfectly and them to figure out something's wrong on their own because that's going to be just relationship destroying

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or QBR rhythm (§9 flags this as a gap). Communication is through Telegram per §5. There is no standing weekly call structure with creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a standing weekly 20-min creator check-in call. Build a lightweight call template in Notion: (1) last-week metrics summary pulled from OF dashboard, (2) content requests for next week, (3) any platform/account issues, (4) open Q&A. Log call notes back to the Creators Airtable record. This directly fills the §9 retention/churn gap.

new_findings.title: Build a model profile doc with chatting style, persona facts, and content inventory for chatters

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

sources.quote: you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's external chat team operates without a documented Palm-side model profile SOP (§8 — outsourced, no internal SOP). Fan Tracker CRM (§9) collects fan data but there is no equivalent creator-side profile that briefs chatters on the model's persona and voice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a 'Model Brief' Airtable record linked to each creator in the Creators table. Fields: chatting tone/style notes, physical stats, hobbies, boundaries, content categories available. Share with external chat team now; becomes the foundation for in-house chatter onboarding when that roadmap item ships (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.title: Request a specific content mix upfront: sexting sets, PPV bundles, mass-message clips, and verification posts

variants.claim: Agencies must tell models exactly what content types they need at onboarding — not generically 'more content' — so all traffic and monetization strategies can be activated immediately.
variants.what_they_do: During the onboarding call they specify four distinct content buckets needed upfront: (1) sexting/script sets for DM selling, (2) PPV bundles and standalone videos for mass messages, (3) SFW promotional content for Reddit, Twitter, and Instagram, (4) verification posts for subreddits that require them. They also provide a weekly content schedule so the model knows what to film the following week.

sources.quote: scripts so like live content that you could sell via sexting sets in the DMS for of you also need to have ppv bundles and and videos that you can use to mass message out
providing some sort of content schedule to them on a weekly basis as to what you would likely need for that following week

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is inspo-driven and strong for social/SFW content. However §2 flags that OF vault/PPV content has no systematic production pipeline. There is no documented process for requesting specific content buckets at onboarding or providing weekly content briefs to creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a content-brief template to the onboarding call checklist: enumerate the four buckets (sexting sets, PPV bundles, SFW promo, verification posts) and send a weekly 'content request' message to each creator. This closes the §2 OF-vault gap and connects to Palm's existing social pipeline for the SFW bucket.

new_findings.title: Coach creators on 4 non-negotiable quality standards: lighting, audio, background, and variety

variants.claim: Agencies should proactively coach creators on production quality during onboarding rather than waiting for bad content to arrive and damage relationships.
variants.what_they_do: They explicitly advise creators on: (1) lighting — minimum ring light or studio light for long-form video, softer/natural for sexting sets to avoid an overly-produced look; (2) audio — no background noise; (3) background — clean, uncluttered; (4) variety — multiple outfits, angles, lingerie styles, and outdoor locations. This is delivered as part of the onboarding process, not as reactive feedback.

sources.quote: lighting is probably the number one most important thing when it comes to taking high quality content make sure that you're using at least a ring light
make sure that the background of the videos and the pictures and everything doesn't have a bunch like a mess or Garbage

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) evaluates *incoming* inspo reels for viral score but there is no documented creator-facing quality brief or production standards SOP. Quality feedback is presumably reactive via the revision workflow (Tasks table).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a one-page 'Content Quality Standards' PDF/Notion doc covering the four pillars above. Send it during onboarding and reference it in weekly content briefs. Add a quality-gate field to the Assets Airtable table so editors can flag substandard submissions for coaching rather than silently fixing or discarding.

new_findings.title: On-shift group call + peer-authority system accelerates collective chatter improvement

variants.claim: An open call for all chatters currently on shift — combined with assigning subject-matter 'authorities' among chatters — creates collaborative learning that makes the whole team improve faster than top-down coaching alone.
variants.what_they_do: Maintain an always-open call channel for chatters on the same shift so they can ask each other for help and share mistakes in real time. Identify each chatter's main strength (e.g., scripting, PPV captioning). When another chatter needs help in that area, route them to the peer expert rather than the manager. This builds internal figures of authority and reduces manager bottleneck.

sources.quote: having an on shift call where the chatters who are currently working can talk to each other
Chatters tend to have one main strength. Identify what that is and then whenever a different chatter asks for help on that topic, you send them to that chatter

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat team structure at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward for in-house team. Relevant also to AI chatting project's team design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When standing up the in-house chat team, create a Telegram or Discord group call as an 'on-shift room.' Map each chatter's strongest skill in Airtable and use that routing table to build peer mentorship from week one.

new_findings.title: Use weekly invoicing explicitly as a trust signal, not just a payment mechanism

variants.claim: Consistent invoicing builds model trust because it creates transparency and accountability, making it irrational for the model to leave a reliable agency over a single payment.
variants.what_they_do: They invoice every creator weekly and explain the invoicing schedule during the onboarding call. They frame invoicing not just as revenue collection but as a trust-building tool — a paper trail that shows the agency is accountable. They also argue that a model generating consistent income has no incentive to leave, reducing churn.

sources.quote: always invoice your clients it's a way to build trust but they're also not going to run if you provide a quality service

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already auto-generates weekly invoices (§7 — multi-account PDF via Puppeteer, Dropbox, Resend). However, Palm's invoicing system is described as an ops tool, not a trust/relationship tool. There is no documented practice of *explaining* the invoicing schedule during onboarding or positioning it as a transparency signal to the creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an 'invoicing walkthrough' step to the onboarding call checklist: show the creator the invoice format, confirm the payment cadence, and explicitly frame it as mutual accountability. This costs nothing — Palm's invoice infrastructure already exists — but closes the relationship-framing gap.

new_findings.title: Price PPVs at .99 endings to exploit charm pricing psychology

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content at $X.99 instead of round numbers (e.g. $49.99 vs $50) makes content feel meaningfully cheaper and increases purchase rates.
variants.what_they_do: Any PPV intended to sell at a round number is repriced to one cent below: $50 → $49.99, $35 → $34.99. This is presented as a direct borrowing of supermarket/retail charm-pricing psychology and is codified as a standing rule in the chatter training guide.

sources.quote: avoid whole numbers when pricing PPVs so if you plan to sell a piece of content for 50 then you'll just reprice it and sell it for $49.99

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). There is no codified rule on round vs. charm pricing. This is a quick, zero-cost win to adopt.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a pricing rule to the future PPV pricing SOP: all PPV prices must end in .99 (never round numbers). Apply immediately when Palm's in-house chat team is stood up, and bake into any AI chatting prompt templates.

new_findings.title: Always wrap up a sexting session before logging off to prevent churn

variants.claim: Abruptly ending a chat session after a subscriber has spent money makes them feel used and dramatically reduces the chance they return or spend again.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly instructed to close every sexting/monetization session with a proper conversational wrap-up — warm sign-off, leaving the subscriber feeling positive and wanting to return. Logging off mid-session is a fireable-level error in the VA training.

sources.quote: always wrap up the conversation sexting session before logging off this is to leave the subscriber feeling good and wanting to come back for more next time otherwise if you abruptly stop replying the subscriber has spent hundreds of dollars on content they're going to feel used

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP. This 'session closure' practice is a key retention mechanic that should be codified in Palm's future in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt instructions.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'session closure protocol' to the chatter SOP: before ending any active conversation, send a warm closing message (e.g. affirmation, teaser for next time). Make this a scored QA checkpoint when reviewing chatter performance.

new_findings.title: Send a single sad-face emoji to re-engage non-responding subscribers

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

sources.quote: if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented re-engagement tactic (§8 outsourced, §9 partial fan retention). This is a high-signal, zero-cost tactic for the in-house chat roadmap and is also relevant to AI chatting logic (trigger sad emoji after N hours of non-response).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this into the chatter re-engagement SOP: if subscriber has not replied after 24–48 hours, send a single sad-face emoji (no text). Log the response rate in Fan Tracker CRM to validate. For AI chatting, add as an automated trigger: no-reply after configurable timeout → send sad emoji → await response before any further action.

new_findings.title: Never send multiple follow-up messages to a non-responding subscriber

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat SOP (§8 outsourced). If the external team lacks this discipline it may be causing churn Palm can't see. This rule should be a core constraint in both the in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt guardrails.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a hard rule to the chatter SOP and AI chatting system prompt: after one unanswered message, no further text messages until the subscriber replies (only the sad-face emoji exception is permitted). Make this a QA audit criterion when reviewing chatter logs.

new_findings.title: Replace rule-sets with 4–5 core values to govern chatter behavior

variants.claim: Values (4–5 core beliefs) outperform rule-sets because they apply to every situation, are easier to enforce, and create a positive work environment.
variants.what_they_do: Agency defines 4–5 values instead of an exhaustive rule list: (1) work for creators / act in their best interest even if it costs money; (2) everyone spends — treat every fan as a potential buyer; (3) make your opportunities — proactively go to fans; (4) play for the team — think agency-wide, not just personal earnings. When a chatter's decision is questioned, the manager asks 'how did that align with our values?' rather than citing a broken rule.

sources.quote: Four to five core beliefs that you want instilled in your chatting team

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chatting team (§8); no internal values framework or SOP exists. This is build-toward intelligence for the planned in-house chat team (~1yr out).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, document 4–5 agency values as the governance layer before writing any procedural rules. Draft values now so they're ready for onboarding the first internal chatter.

consensus_risers.title: Promote staff up the five levels of accountability to remove yourself as bottleneck

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.
delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no hiring scorecards, accountability framework, or management cadence documented (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the five-level accountability ladder as a management standard, push leads to level 4-5 with SOP-building expectations, and run Mon-Fri morning leadership calls.

consensus_risers.title: Qualify creators against a checklist and disqualify on red flags

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

variants.claim: How a model responds during onboarding — responsiveness, receptiveness to feedback, implementation of early requests — is a leading indicator of her long-term revenue potential and whether the agency should continue investing in her.
variants.what_they_do: They observe model behavior during the onboarding phase as a quality signal: slow responses, failure to implement feedback, or resistance to criticism are treated as early warnings that the creator will not reach top-tier earnings. They acknowledge that new agencies must work with whoever they can get but advise building judgment over time to screen for quality clients.

variants.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
variants.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.

sources.quote: Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.
if they're flaky or they don't respond very good or they take forever to respond or they don't Implement criticism you most likely don't have a very high quality client
work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding captures admin fields but has no documented fit scorecard or red-flag screen (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an ideal-client checklist plus a red-flag screen to the onboarding/intake step so Palm signs for fit and retention, not just availability.

consensus_risers.title: Make the fan chase to hold power

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait
in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information

palm_comparison.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

consensus_risers.title: Central account/team/finance trackers to remove owner from ops

variants.claim: A scaled agency runs on connected central databases (accounts, team, content, finance) so operational detail lives in systems, not the owner's head.
variants.what_they_do: Runs six linked Notion systems (model account manager with logins/warmup SOPs, VA/team tracker with pay+KPIs, content HQ, finance tracker, iPhone tracker, AI to-do list) that interlink so the owner only reviews and decides.

variants.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.

variants.claim: A six-figure operation runs on a defined low-cost software stack: fan CRM, video-call tool, deep-link redirect, editor, anti-detect browser, and Telegram for coordination.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Infloww (fan CRM/chatting/scripts, priced as a capped revenue percentage), Google Meet (model calls), Bouncy.ai deep links to hide OF URLs from Meta/Snap, CapCut (editing), Dolphin Anty anti-detect browser + proxies for Reddit, and Telegram with per-topic subgroups to run the whole team.

sources.quote: the model account manager
the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table
what we do is we do this thing called a deep link
the most important one... will be Telegram

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is already strong here — Airtable HQ + Content Ops, editor task queue, invoicing, whale CRM (§1/§7/§9). Validates Palm's systemization; the gap is a unified creator-phase/KPI rollup.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a single creator-phase + KPI rollup (revenue, chatting ratio, follower growth, account status) across Palm's Airtable bases, surfaced in the SMM hub Overview.

consensus_risers.title: Rapport, transition, then sell

variants.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard trains chatters on a fixed framework (general conversation/rapport, transition to a sexual conversation, then dirty talk leading into PPV) and lets the customer initiate so the model holds the power position; habibi similarly builds rapport and conserves energy rather than blasting horny messages or mass-message bundles.

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: Step One is General conversation building report... step two is transitioning to a more sexual conversation
conserving your energy you should never be throwing all of your energy away
the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced; Palm does not own script flow (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward for the in-house and AI chat roadmap. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a mandated rapport-then-transition-then-sell chat framework as the backbone script when chatting comes in-house or is automated.

consensus_risers.title: Revenue-gated hiring ladder: VAs, then chat manager, then ops

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you need to hire managers
10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.
you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin with no documented hiring criteria, comp bands, or scaling plan (§10). This is a ready-made staffing/comp ladder.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this ladder as a template for Palm's org-scaling plan; map Palm roles (Amin, editors, chat) onto the stages and define revenue gates for next hires.

consensus_risers.title: Build a fan profile through scripted questions before any PPV

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

variants.claim: DM selling is treated as gamified casino psychology where the chatter maintains the power dynamic, captures fan data for rapport, and never breaks the fantasy by talking price.
variants.what_they_do: Auto-welcome DM collects name/age/interests into fan notes for later rapport (asking about a fan's dog by name); avoid being beggy or discussing price; price-locked content keeps fans from noticing spend; keep the model 'the prize' and just out of reach; deny AI accusations and use new content plus voice notes to rebut.

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

sources.quote: what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200
in a casino they do everything to make you blind to the fact you're there losing all your money
use fan notes to store this information
that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but no chatting scripts or data-capture SOP (§8 OUTSOURCED).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pair Palm's fan CRM with a scripted data-collection intake and a build-up PPV ladder for the in-house/AI chat build.

consensus_risers.title: Pre-built escalating sexting scripts are the sales unit

variants.claim: Top sellers sell DM content via pre-produced, internally-consistent escalation scripts that simulate a live session and raise price as explicitness increases.
variants.what_they_do: Each 'sexting script' is a fixed 4-step bundle (SFW starter, first-tease, jugs-only, full-nude bundle) with 2-3 variations, all shot in one consistent setting/lighting; price escalates per step; ElevenLabs voice notes add realism; script content is never reused in mass-PPV or fans realize it is not live.

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
variants.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.

sources.quote: one fully clothed safer work script starter
use 11 Labs to generate super realistic notes
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels
it's easy to replicate you just have one script ready and you can sell it to every single subscriber
ideally the subscriber gets more and more horny he likes the script more and more he buys everything

palm_comparison.vs_us: OF vault/PPV is Palm's thinnest area (§2 THIN, no system) and chatting is outsourced (§8 OUTSOURCED); no documented PPV bundle or script structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the escalating-bundle plus consistency rules as the core spec for Palm's future AI-chat and in-house vault production; AI voice-note generation overlaps the active AI-chatting project.

consensus_risers.title: 7-day IG warm-up: algorithm training, niche engagement, delayed link

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

sources.quote: how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently
never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature and fully specified (§4 90-day cadence) but real-creator warmup is a documented gap (§4 THIN); this gives a concrete short-form warmup playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt a documented short warm-up + delayed-link checklist for new real-creator IG accounts (algorithm training, niche engagement, no-DM rule, risk-to-reward link timing) to fill the §4 real-creator gap.

consensus_risers.title: Replace yourself via ownership ladder, not task delegation

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

variants.claim: Scale past a plateau by training department heads to own problems end-to-end rather than escalating every decision to the owner.
variants.what_they_do: Five accountability levels: (1) wait to be told, (2) bring the problem, (3) bring problem + 1-2 solutions, (4) solve it and report, (5) solve it AND build an SOP so it never recurs. Most teams sit at 1-2, making the owner the bottleneck. Push C-suite heads to level 4-5; example: an acquisition manager independently bought aged warmed IG accounts and hired another VA to lift outreach capacity, then just reported it. Hold Mon-Fri morning team calls with only the department heads.

sources.quote: delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation
there are five main levels of accountability... the first level being they just simply wait for the command on what to do.
what's different between level five and level four is they not only find the problem and solve it, but they also will build infrastructure to ensure that this problem never happens again.
making sure that you guys do morning team calls Monday to Friday.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10 GAP): no org chart, ownership model, or decision framework; founder/tech concentrated on Josh and Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ownership-ladder + decision-filter operating model so Palm functions (acquisition, content, chat, ops) each have an owner and decisions don't route through the founders — directly feeds the teammate-org initiative.

consensus_risers.title: Tag every fan by relationship type and price accordingly

variants.claim: High-converting chat operations segment every subscriber into named relationship/spender tiers and differentiate content, pricing and chatter effort by tier.
variants.what_they_do: Four fan types (transactional, lover, whale, time-waster) marked with emoji labels in Fanvue fan-notes and placed into platform lists; mass-PPV sent per list excluding whales; same content sold at different prices by tier; whales sold to 1:1 at high anchors, never via mass message.

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: Mark all spenders on relationship types
Target each group with specific mass messages
people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has Fan Tracker/whale-hunting data collection (§9 PARTIAL) but chatting is outsourced (§8) with no in-house fan-tiering SOP or differential-pricing model; pricing is also a gap (§7).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a fan-tier taxonomy plus differential-PPV pricing ladder into the in-house/AI chatting roadmap, fed from existing Fan Tracker data now; pricing applies to real creators today.

consensus_risers.title: Price dynamically off purchase history

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced
you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

consensus_risers.title: High daily posting cadence and brand decay

variants.claim: Brand equity decays with every silent day, so consistent daily multi-post output across platforms is required maintenance, not optional.
variants.what_they_do: Posts 6x/day across IG and TikTok personally; frames missed days as compound interest in reverse where creators, algorithms, and the market all forget; only tracks posting consistency (plus inbounds, repeat viewers, proof) as a real KPI, ignoring vanity metrics.

variants.claim: Reels are the primary growth lever and each is engineered with a 3-second hook, mid retention and a payoff, produced at 2-3/day split across a few fixed content pillars.
variants.what_they_do: Treats reels as the top of the IG pecking order (reels > posts > stories > funnel); structures each as hook (first 3s) / retention / reward (e.g. an ugly-to-hot transformation); defines ~3 niche content pillars and split-tests them at 2-3 reels/day, doubling down on winners and cutting losers; moved from static text-on-image reels to 'clean motion' video.

sources.quote: I post six times a day
every time that you stop posting for a week, your brand equity decays
Every reel should have a hook, a retention and a reward
a minimum of two quality reels per day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's real-creator posting cadence (frequency, time-of-day) is explicitly not codified (§1/§4 GAP); AI-account warmup cadence is fully specified (§4 MATURE).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator posting cadence (target posts/day, consistency tracking) in the SMM hub, mirroring the rigor already applied to AI-account warmup.

consensus_risers.title: Source creators across IG, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok, and prioritize referrals

variants.claim: Find creators by searching niche keywords across multiple platforms, but treat satisfied-client referrals as the highest-quality channel.
variants.what_they_do: Search 'only fans' / niche terms (e.g. gamer girl) on Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, TikTok and filter for link-in-bio + recent activity. TikTok surfaces up-and-comers; Reddit is where whales and creators already are. Best channel = referrals: after a great first month in the green, ask the happy creator who else does OnlyFans.

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: first one of course is Instagram... Your other option is going to be Twitter... The next one is going to be Reddit... You can also find them on Tik Tok.
by far the best is referrals... after you've delivered a great service... ask, do you know of anybody else who's also doing Only Fans.
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already uses referrals (§6) but has no documented multi-platform sourcing process or a systematic referral ask.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-platform sourcing process and bake a structured referral ask into the post-first-month creator-success cadence.

consensus_risers.title: Weekly cadence and standards prevent regression to chaos

variants.claim: A fixed weekly operating cadence plus enforced standards (recognition and consequences) keeps an agency from regressing into reactive firefighting.
variants.what_they_do: Installs a Monday hour-one reset (look back, look forward, pick 1-3 priorities, schedule, overflow), a weekly single 'game tape' root-cause review, decision-only compressed 30-min meetings, and a recognition/consequence system; 'culture is what you tolerate and what you reinforce.'

variants.claim: Top operators run a weekly operating system that constantly adds new PPV scripts and prunes underperformers by analytics so whales never exhaust the content.
variants.what_they_do: Weekly: add 1-2 new sexting scripts, generate fresh wall teasers, log what content/price performs, replace underperformers (scripts that cause unsubs), keep the vault on-niche and price-test ranges — because a whale burns through 3-4 scripts fast and an empty vault leaves money on the table.

sources.quote: Without rhythm, everything will regress into chaos
Culture is what you tolerate and culture is what you reinforce
You need to always stay on top of refreshing your scripts
a high quality vault from day one

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm coordinates via Telegram with no documented operating cadence, scorecards, or standards-enforcement rhythm (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Install a lightweight weekly cadence (priorities reset + one root-cause review) and a recognition/consequence standard across Palm's team and contractors.

consensus_risers.title: AI personas remove model-dependency risk and keep 100% of revenue

variants.claim: Operators argue an owned synthetic creator beats managing real talent because it removes model-dependency, agency-hopping and split-revenue risk while keeping all earnings.
variants.what_they_do: Frames traditional OFM as a 'casino' (lazy models, theft, unpaid invoices, agency hoppers) where a $24k month nets only ~$6.5k after split; an AI model on Fanvue keeps 100% (minus 20% platform fee) for ~$17.5k net, fully owner-controlled and self-reliant.

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

sources.quote: you get 100% share obviously with this business model
It's a casino because that's basically what it is
they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs real creators on OnlyFans today with revenue split (§0) and treats AI personas as a future scaling lever (§3 MATURE). Palm's AI personas still funnel to OnlyFans via social, unlike the AI-creator/Fanvue model taught here.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold for the future AI-creator line: validate the owner-keeps-100% Fanvue economics as the business case for a Palm AI line, separate from the real-creator OnlyFans core.

consensus_risers.title: A defined niche, not looks or tech, separates earners

variants.claim: The biggest driver of five-figure outcomes is a hyper-specific model niche selling a defined fantasy and connection, not production quality or attractiveness.
variants.what_they_do: Reject 'basic blonde' generic models; build niche verticals (nurse, air-hostess, alt/gamer, ethnicity, trans) so 'fans feel she's for them', raising perceived value and scarcity; enforce that the niche/branding stays consistent across bio, highlights, reels, names and the Fanvue vault.

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: It's the niche
you're selling connection and fantasy
within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1 MATURE) decides what to film from viral data but is not framed around per-creator niche/fantasy positioning; positioning is implicit, not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an explicit niche/positioning step to the inspo pipeline for real creators and enforce cross-platform branding consistency; high-value overlap with current work.

consensus_risers.title: Run a multi-platform traffic mix; no single source carries it

variants.claim: Reliable revenue requires combining several traffic sources rather than relying on one, with each platform graded on traffic quality, not raw views.
variants.what_they_do: Recommends Instagram (primary, highest-quality subs), Threads, and X together, with TikTok optional; X drives high volume but lower-quality subs; explicitly moves away from pure rage-bait because '28.5M views' does not equal money — quality and niche of traffic matter for conversion.

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: you need to be using a combination of all different traffic sources
it doesn't always mean views equals money
are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-Amin posting (§5 PARTIAL) with traffic strategy across channels undocumented (§5 gap).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a documented multi-channel traffic mix per creator with per-channel quality grading, so channel investment is steered by conversion quality not view counts.

consensus_risers.title: Do every role yourself first, document it, then hand it off

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.
It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on productized content tooling (§1) but has no stated document-before-delegate discipline; team/org THIN (§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize SOP + Loom documentation per role before backfilling it, hire for coachability, and attach KPIs to each function.

consensus_risers.title: AI-image watermarks (SynthID) are the new ban trigger; switch generators

variants.claim: Platforms now algorithmically detect AI-generated images via invisible watermarks/metadata, so operators must avoid watermarking generators and look human to survive ban waves.
variants.what_they_do: Blames a wave of Instagram bans on Nano Banana Pro images carrying Google SynthID + metadata that Meta detects and flags as fraud/deception; fix is to generate with tools that leave no SynthID/metadata, avoid OF/Fanvue logos and links in bio, list multiple socials to look human, and use lower-profile link providers (Linktree over flagged Link.me).

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: Google Nano Banana Pro has, what we discussed earlier, something called a Synth ID
the most common ban reason is fraud and deception
there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI program is EU AI Act compliant with required AI label + watermark (§3), the opposite stance; for real creators Palm has no documented platform-risk/ban-avoidance policy (§4/§5 gaps).
palm_comparison.recommendation: AI line: note label/watermark compliance is in direct tension with the ban-evasion tactics gurus use, so plan for platform-compliant distribution. Real creators: adopt the human-looking-bio + safe link-provider hygiene now.

consensus_risers.title: Run three departments (acquisition, marketing, chatting) each under a manager

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

variants.claim: Hires should be added on a revenue-gated ladder — owner does everything to ~5k, VAs/chatters at 5-20k, managers at 20k+, ops director at 50-100k+.
variants.what_they_do: 0-5k owner solo; 5-20k first VA/chatter (after chatting yourself 2 weeks); 20-40k chat manager first then content/account managers; 50-100k+ ops director to execute proven systems so the owner exits day-to-day.

sources.quote: you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.
you need to hire managers

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm org is THIN (§10): no management layer between founders and ICs, no org chart.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a department-and-manager org (acquisition / marketing / chatting) with the founder managing through managers as Palm scales past the current flat structure.

consensus_risers.title: Convert founder gut-reactions into SOPs so the team executes your standards without you

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

sources.quote: if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP
you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's systems are strong on the content pipeline (§1) but thin on creator-facing ops, chatting protocols, and acquisition handling (§6, §8). The SOP layer for creator management and issue resolution is not documented internally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Run a structured 'intuition capture' session: Josh walks through the 10 most common creator/fan/operational situations he handles and dictates how he'd resolve each. Evan documents these as v1 SOPs in Airtable or Notion. Prioritize creator onboarding issues, escalation handling, and any workflow currently bottlenecked on Josh.

consensus_risers.title: Snapchat + read-only Telegram channel as a ban-resilient backup funnel

variants.claim: Operators add low-effort Snapchat reach and an owned Telegram broadcast channel to nurture leads and survive social bans, since owned audiences persist when accounts die.
variants.what_they_do: ~15 min/day Snapchat: public profile, repurposed 9:16 content, 2-3 stories/day, Telegram CTA only once/week at peak time, no DMs/links to avoid bans; funnels into a read-only Telegram broadcast channel (pinned Fanvue link, niche real-life teasing, reaction-bait) that both converts and acts as a 'safe haven' to relaunch socials after a ban.
