variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: Over here we have the Telegram funnel
builds a safe haven for leads on your Telegram if a social ban occurs
boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is Publer + Telegram-to-poster (§5) with no documented owned-audience/backup-channel or Snapchat strategy; traffic strategy is a gap (§5).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot an owned Telegram (or email) audience as a ban-resilient backup channel for real creators, plus Snapchat as a cheap secondary funnel; reduces single-platform dependency risk.

consensus_risers.topic: Revenue vs profit distinction at scale
consensus_risers.title: High revenue means nothing without margin — $100k revenue can yield $3k profit

variants.claim: Agencies can hit $100,000/month in revenue while netting as little as $3,000 in profit, so raw revenue targets are misleading without tracking margin.
variants.what_they_do: TDM explicitly warns against 'brute forcing' to $50–70k/month and treating it as success; they frame margin as the true health metric, arguing that low-margin agencies cannot reinvest in better team, systems, or experimentation, which caps growth.

variants.claim: Allocating resources by cost-per-acquisition versus LTV and target margin, rather than by follower counts or a raw LTV number, is how a scaled agency decides where to invest.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media tracks free and paid LTV over 7 and 30 days against a roughly $1 Reddit CAC, targets a 50% margin on base traffic (accepting 20-30% on low-effort purchased traffic), and color-codes models so investment flows to the highest CAC:LTV returns; the founder calls 'what is a good LTV' the wrong question.

sources.quote: some agencies will do $100,000 in revenue and $3,000 in profit
we are always monitoring free LTV and paid LTV over a 7-day and 30-day period
what is a good LTV... it's just the wrong question... what we actually care about is cac to ltv

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented internal baseline for margin targets or profit-vs-revenue tracking (§7 GAP). Weekly invoices are auto-computed but there is no documented P&L framework or margin floor that triggers a scaling decision.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a minimum margin % threshold (e.g., 30–40% net) as an internal KPI alongside gross revenue. Build a simple P&L view into the admin portal or Airtable so Josh and Evan can see whether revenue growth is actually profitable before committing to new hires or infrastructure.

consensus_risers.topic: Content repurposing — structural uniqueness to evade IG detection
consensus_risers.title: Make repurposed Reels structurally different, not just color-adjusted

variants.claim: Changing only brightness/contrast is insufficient to fool Instagram's duplicate-detection; the video's structure must be genuinely altered.
variants.what_they_do: They download the original Reel watermark-free (SnapTick/SnapInsta), then apply structural edits in any editing app: trim 0.1s from a scene cut, mirror the clip, insert a different opening scene, and also adjust color/contrast slightly — the goal is a video that IS different, not merely looks different.

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: you're going to take 0.1 second out of one scene. You're going to basically mirror the video, right? You're going to add a different scene at the beginning
there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces organic, creator-filmed Reels and does not currently have a repurposing workflow. The structural-edit technique is a distinct add-on to the existing pipeline for any scaled reposting layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm ever runs multi-account reposting for a single creator, document a mandatory structural-edit checklist (scene trim, mirror, new intro scene + minor color grade) before export. Add this as a step in the existing pipeline for repurposed variants, distinct from the organic primary post.

consensus_risers.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

variants.claim: Every time the founder instinctively knows how they would handle a situation, that instinct should be written into an SOP so the team follows the same standard without requiring the founder's involvement.
variants.what_they_do: TDM's framework: 'your intuition, the way you feel, should become the rules of the business.' If the founder would handle a creator issue a certain way, that becomes a documented framework the team is trained on. The goal is to remove the founder from execution while preserving their operating philosophy.

sources.quote: Loom recordings
if you feel that, oh, I would deal with a creator's issue like this, then that needs to become an SOP

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

consensus_risers.title: Give every model a written character

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

sources.quote: you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them
make it that her persona

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline drives content from viral inspo data (§1) but there is no documented per-creator character/persona layer tying content to a sales story. Gap on branding/type-2.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a per-creator character sheet (persona, backstory, target fan demographic) that both content and the chat team work from, so content ladders into monetization.

new_evidence.title: Never link OF/Fanvue directly; warm accounts before linking

variants.claim: To avoid bans, operators never post adult-platform links directly on social and warm new accounts before adding any link-in-bio CTA.
variants.what_they_do: Always route via a neutral landing page (e.g. Get All My Links) since a reported direct OF/Fanvue link can trigger strikes/bans; spend the first ~30 days on profile setup, spaced posting and trust-score building before putting links in bio; avoid rapid-burst follows/comments and automation that trip platform limits.

variants.claim: New social accounts should be warmed as normal users and post content for about two weeks before adding the OnlyFans link to protect trust score.
variants.what_they_do: Acts as a normal user (scroll/like, follow niche on day 2, don't post day 1), waits ~2 weeks of posting before adding any link, and pre-builds accounts before signing a client so revenue never stalls.

variants.claim: New social accounts are warmed for about a week of human-like activity and niche engagement before any adult link is added, to build trust score and avoid early bans.
variants.what_they_do: Day-by-day: matching Gmail/name, scroll+like niche reels to train the algorithm, neutral (non-model) profile pic, follow ~15 niche creators/day, engage and like older US/UK 'simp' commenters under competitor posts to pull them over (never DM), then post first reel ~day 7 and only add the link.bio funnel 2-4 weeks in when followers justify the risk.

sources.quote: never post direct to Fan View. Always use the Get All My Links
warm up slowly, act like a human
post content for 2 weeks before adding a link
how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI warmup is mature with a 90-day cadence, Day-21 link handoff and Day-45 OF-CTA gate (§4), but real-creator warmup and link-safety rules are a gap (§4 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a real-creator account-warmup plus landing-page-only link policy mirroring the AI warmup gates (§4) to reduce strike/ban risk on creator socials.

_new_sources.quote: how you set up and warm up an Instagram properly within 7 days to avoid being banned
Build up your Instagram account slowly and patiently

new_evidence.title: Use an Intrigue-Curiosity-CTA opener and book the call fast

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

sources.quote: I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.
most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented DM script, send-rate discipline, or follow-up cadence for acquisition (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ICC opener template, a manual-typed/personalized/spaced sending SOP to avoid bans, and a 2-week daily follow-up rule; track per-step conversion.

_new_sources.quote: most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.

new_evidence.title: Cold outreach is a volume-consistency-conversation game

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

variants.claim: Outreach should be tracked stage-by-stage in a daily spreadsheet so the bleeding step is fixed one variable at a time after a meaningful sample.
variants.what_they_do: Logs source link, DMs sent, replies, qualified convos, asks-for-call, booked/showed/closed per channel; waits ~3,000 DMs before changing anything, then changes one variable at a time. Uses a 7-12 message value-first follow-up chain (most deals close on touch 5-7) including a 'red flag' breakup follow-up with ~95% response.

sources.quote: the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text
after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led but not run as a tracked, committed-volume system (§6 THIN); no defined daily quota or multi-account structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize Palm outbound as a committed daily-volume system (per-day DM quota, multi-account, voice/video openers, qualify-in-conversation) rather than ad-hoc DMs.

_new_sources.quote: after about 3,000 DMs... you'll know which layer of your system is actually bleeding
Most deals happen between the fifth and seventh touch point

new_evidence.title: An AI chatbot can replace much of the chat team at a fraction of the cost

variants.claim: A well-configured AI chatting bot can handle 24/7 fan conversations and PPV upsells at a fraction of a chat team's cost, but only if it is carefully set up and trained.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Super Creator's 'Izzy' AI chatbot (~$200-300/month vs a full chatting team), configured with extensive persona data, slang, pricing strategy, and trained on rapport/texting/selling scenarios; warns that a bad setup just creates 'a shitty chatter'.

variants.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
variants.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.

sources.quote: it only costs like $2 $300 a month, which is pennies compared to how much you're forking out... on chatters
if you don't set it up correctly, you are simply going to make a shitty chatter
I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

palm_comparison.vs_us: Directly maps to Palm's active AI-chatting project (§8 build-toward): defines a concrete tool pattern and the heavy-configuration requirement.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a configurable AI chat bot (Super Creator-class) for Palm's AI-chatting project, budgeting for heavy persona setup/training rather than expecting plug-and-play.

_new_sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients

new_evidence.title: Done beats perfect; minimize record-to-publish friction

variants.claim: Reducing friction between idea and published content matters more than production polish, because perfection kills the frequency that actually compounds.
variants.what_they_do: Films unedited YouTube videos from bullet points with just a mic and DJI Osmo + Screenflow, uses 4-5 templated thumbnails, and delegates only short-form editing; films an idea the moment it strikes rather than deferring it.

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: perfection is what kills frequency. So done always beats perfect
I just like to reduce the friction between me having an idea and then making content
volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's editor workflow is heavy and approval-gated (§1: 2 slots/day per creator, revisions) — appropriate for monetizable social assets but slow for founder/brand content.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For any Palm/founder brand content, run a low-friction lane (bullet points, templated thumbnails, minimal editing) separate from the polished creator-asset pipeline.

_new_sources.quote: volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

new_evidence.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.
charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

new_evidence.topic: Multi-account setup — preventing Instagram account linkage
new_evidence.title: Differentiate every signal across repurposing accounts to prevent IG cluster-ban

variants.claim: Instagram actively clusters accounts that share profile pictures, sounds, bios, links, and feed content, then bans them as a group.
variants.what_they_do: Repurposing accounts must have: completely different usernames (not sequential variations), different profile pictures, different feed posts, different bios, and different funneling links (Beacons vs Linktree vs direct, etc.). The only common element is the creator's face in video content.

variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

sources.quote: Different profile pictures, different usernames… different feed posts, different bio, maybe a different funneling setup

if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third
you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-account warmup system (§3/§4) already enforces hardware and SIM isolation per persona, and uses dedicated aged Gmail accounts. However, Palm's documented warmup is for AI personas, not for multi-account real-creator repurposing. The same differentiation logic should apply if Palm ever runs repurposing account clusters for real creators.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm builds a repurposing layer for real creators, extend the existing AI-account differentiation playbook (§3/§4) to cover: username diversity rules, bio templates that vary per account, and link rotation (alternate between Beacons, Linktree, direct OF link). No sequential naming conventions.

new_evidence.title: Target mid-tier creators (10K followers, ~$1-3K/mo) not top earners

variants.claim: Beginner agencies should target creators with ~10-15K followers already making $1-3K/month rather than chasing large verified accounts who won't respond.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly filter outreach to creators in the 10K-15K follower range earning roughly $1-3K/month on OF, on the basis that they are more open to support, more reachable via DM, and more likely to convert to a managed relationship.

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: targeting more beginner friendly creators as well. Also, someone that's kind of starting out, 10,000 15,000 followers, ones that are also making around 10K a month or so
you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). No internal baseline exists for follower count, current earnings, or engagement thresholds that qualify a creator for outreach.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum viable creator profile for outreach: e.g., 10K-20K IG followers, $1-3K/mo existing OF revenue, active posting cadence. Add these as qualification fields to the creator onboarding form or a pre-onboarding screening step in Airtable.

new_evidence.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator
Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs a recreation pipeline for AI personas funneling to OnlyFans (§3), but does NOT yet clone REAL creators to scale their social volume. This is a direct extension of Palm's existing AI-persona capability onto the real-creator core business (§0/§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot cloning one cooperative real creator: train an AI likeness, post a real/AI mix to her existing funnel, keep all OF vault content 100% real. Leverage Palm's existing recreation pipeline.

new_evidence.title: Main brand account fed by disposable burner accounts

variants.claim: On X, a protected main brand account funneled by several disposable high-volume burner accounts maximizes growth while insulating the asset from bans.
variants.what_they_do: Runs one main account (2-3 quality posts/day, funnels to OF) plus 3-5 burner accounts (3-4 aggressive trend posts/day) that spam trends and pin replies funneling all traffic to the main; if a burner is banned, it's disposable.

variants.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
variants.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.

sources.quote: These are your growth engine
the content designed to get replies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented traffic/distribution strategy for X (§5 gap). This is a concrete account-architecture pattern Palm lacks entirely.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot a main+burner X structure for one creator; treat burners as expendable growth engines and the main as the OF-converting asset.
